modules.explanation: Ordered pairs are like coordinates on a map - the order matters! (2,3) is different from (3,2). Cartesian product combines two sets to create all possible ordered pairs, like pairing every shirt with every pant in your wardrobe. If Set A has 3 elements and Set B has 4 elements, their Cartesian product A×B has 3×4=12 ordered pairs. This concept is fundamental for understanding relations and functions!

questions.question_text: What is an ordered pair?
options: A pair of elements where order matters
A pair of elements where order doesn't matter
questions.correct_answer: A pair of elements where order matters
questions.rationale: In an ordered pair (a,b), the position of a and b is significant.

questions.question_text: If A = {1, 2} and B = {3, 4}, what is A × B?
questions.rationale: Cartesian product A×B pairs each element of A with each element of B.

questions.question_text: If A has 3 elements and B has 4 elements, how many ordered pairs are in A × B?
What is B × A if A = {1, 2} and B = {3, 4}?
questions.rationale: B × A pairs each element of B with each element of A.

questions.question_text: When is A × B equal to B × A?
options: When A and B have one element each
questions.rationale: A × B = B × A only when sets A and B are equal.

questions.question_text: If A = ∅ and B = {1, 2, 3}, what is A × B?
questions.rationale: Cartesian product with empty set is always empty set.

questions.question_text: What is the first element of the ordered pair (5,8)?
questions.rationale: In ordered pair (a,b), a is the first element.

questions.question_text: If A = {a, b} and B = {1, 2, 3}, how many elements are in A × B?
Which coordinate represents the point where x=4 and y=2?
questions.rationale: Point with x-coordinate 4 and y-coordinate 2 is (4,2).

questions.question_text: What is the Cartesian product of {1} and {a, b, c}?
How many ordered pairs are possible if you have 4 shirts and 3 pants?
If |A| = 5 and |B| = 7, how many ordered pairs in A × B?
What is the second element of the ordered pair (9,6)?
questions.rationale: In ordered pair (a,b), b is the second element.

questions.question_text: If A = {0,1} and B = {2,3,4}, what is A × B?
questions.rationale: A × B pairs each element of A with each element of B.

questions.question_text: Which ordered pair represents coordinates (x,y) = (-2,5)?
questions.rationale: Ordered pair with first element -2 and second element 5.

modules.explanation: Relations are like connections between people in a social network! A relation is a subset of Cartesian product that shows which pairs are 'related' or connected. For example, in the relation 'is less than' from {1,2,3} to {4,5,6}, only pairs like (1,4), (1,5), (1,6), (2,4), (2,5), (2,6), (3,4), (3,5), (3,6) are included. Relations help us understand how elements from different sets are connected!

questions.question_text: What is a relation?
questions.rationale: A relation is any subset of the Cartesian product of two sets.

questions.question_text: If A = {1, 2, 3} and B = {4, 5}, which of these is a valid relation from A to B?
questions.rationale: A relation from A to B must have ordered pairs where first element is from A and second from B.

questions.question_text: What is the domain of the relation R = {(1,4), (2,5), (3,6)}?
questions.rationale: Domain is the set of all first elements of the ordered pairs.

questions.question_text: What is the range of the relation R = {(1,4), (2,5), (3,6)}?
questions.rationale: Range is the set of all second elements of the ordered pairs.

questions.question_text: How many relations are possible from a set with 2 elements to a set with 3 elements?
questions.rationale: Total possible ordered pairs = 2 × 3 = 6. Number of relations = 2^6 = 64.

questions.question_text: What is the empty relation?
options: A relation with no ordered pairs
A relation with all ordered pairs
A relation with one ordered pair
A relation that is a function
questions.correct_answer: A relation with no ordered pairs
questions.rationale: Empty relation contains no ordered pairs - it's the empty set.

questions.question_text: What is the universal relation?
options: A relation containing all ordered pairs
A relation with no ordered pairs
A relation with one ordered pair
A relation that is a function
questions.correct_answer: A relation containing all ordered pairs
questions.rationale: Universal relation contains all possible ordered pairs from A × B.

questions.question_text: If R = {(x,y) | x² = y} where x ∈ {1,2,3}, what is R?
If R = {(1,2), (1,3), (2,4)}, is R a function?
questions.rationale: R is not a function because 1 is related to both 2 and 3.

questions.question_text: What is the inverse relation of R = {(1,4), (2,5), (3,6)}?
questions.rationale: Inverse relation swaps each ordered pair: (a,b) becomes (b,a).

questions.question_text: If A = {1,2,3,4} and R = {(a,b) | a divides b}, which pair is in R?
questions.rationale: 2 divides 4 (4 ÷ 2 = 2), so (2,4) is in the relation.

questions.question_text: What is the identity relation on set A = {1,2,3}?
questions.rationale: Identity relation contains all pairs where an element is related to itself.

questions.question_text: If R = {(a,b) | a > b} where a,b ∈ {1,2,3,4}, how many pairs are in R?
If R = {(a,b) | a + b ≤ 5} where a,b ∈ ℕ, which pair is NOT in R?
questions.rationale: 3 + 3 = 6 > 5, so (3,3) is not in the relation.

questions.question_text: How many relations are possible from set A to itself if |A| = 3?
questions.rationale: A × A has 3 × 3 = 9 ordered pairs. Number of relations = 2^9 = 512.

modules.explanation: Functions are like special machines - you put in an input and get exactly one output! A function is a relation where each element in the domain is paired with exactly one element in the range. Think of a vending machine: press button A (input) and you always get the same snack (output). Functions are everywhere - from calculating grades to predicting weather, they help us understand how one quantity depends on another!

questions.question_text: What is the defining property of a function?
options: Each input has exactly one output
Each output has exactly one input
questions.correct_answer: Each input has exactly one output
questions.rationale: A function assigns exactly one output to each input in its domain.

questions.question_text: Which of these relations is a function?
questions.rationale: Each input (1,2,3) has exactly one output, satisfying the function definition.

questions.question_text: What is the domain of f(x) = 2x + 1?
questions.rationale: For any real x, 2x + 1 is defined, so domain is all real numbers.

questions.question_text: What is the range of f(x) = x² where x ∈ ℝ?
questions.rationale: x² is always ≥ 0 for real x, so range is [0, ∞).

questions.question_text: If f(x) = 3x - 2, what is f(4)?
What type of function is f(x) = 2x + 3?
questions.rationale: f(x) = 2x + 3 has the form ax + b, which is a linear function.

questions.question_text: What is the domain of f(x) = 1/(x-2)?
questions.rationale: Division by zero is undefined, so x-2 ≠ 0, meaning x ≠ 2.

questions.question_text: If f(x) = x² and g(x) = x + 1, what is f(g(2))?
Which function has a parabola as its graph?
questions.rationale: Quadratic functions like x² have parabolic graphs.

questions.question_text: What is the range of f(x) = -x² + 4?
questions.rationale: Since coefficient of x² is negative, parabola opens downward with maximum at y = 4.

questions.question_text: If f: ℝ → ℝ is defined by f(x) = 2x + 5, what is f⁻¹(x)?
questions.rationale: To find inverse: y = 2x + 5. Solve for x: x = (y - 5)/2. So f⁻¹(x) = (x - 5)/2.

questions.question_text: What type of function is f(x) = 1/x?
questions.rationale: f(x) = 1/x is a reciprocal function, also called hyperbola.

questions.question_text: If f(x) = x³, what is the range when domain is ℝ?
questions.rationale: Cubic function x³ takes all real values as x varies over real numbers.

questions.question_text: What is the composition (f∘g)(x) if f(x) = x + 2 and g(x) = 3x?
Which function is one-to-one but not onto from ℝ to ℝ?
questions.rationale: eˣ is one-to-one but its range is (0, ∞), not all ℝ, so not onto.

modules.explanation: Arithmetic progressions are like climbing stairs with equal steps! Each term increases or decreases by the same amount called the common difference. If you start at 3 and add 2 each time: 3, 5, 7, 9, 11... that's an AP! APs help model situations with steady growth like salary increases, monthly savings, or regular payment plans. The nth term formula an = a₁ + (n-1)d helps find any term without listing all!

questions.question_text: What is an arithmetic progression?
questions.rationale: AP has constant difference between consecutive terms.

questions.question_text: What is the common difference of AP: 3, 7, 11, 15?
What is the 10th term of AP: 2, 5, 8, 11...?
Which sequence is an AP?
If a₁ = 5 and d = 3, what is a₇?
What is the formula for nth term of AP?
questions.rationale: Standard formula for nth term of arithmetic progression.

questions.question_text: Which term is 35 in AP: 5, 8, 11, 14...?
What is the sum of first 10 terms of AP: 1, 4, 7, 10...?
If a₁ = 10 and a₁₀ = 55, what is d?
What type of AP has negative common difference?
questions.rationale: Negative common difference means terms decrease.

questions.question_text: If a₅ = 20 and a₁₀ = 45, what is a₁?
What is the middle term of AP with 9 terms: 2, 5, 8...?
If three terms of AP are a, b, c, what is the relationship?
options: Both 2b = a + c and b = (a + c)/2
questions.correct_answer: Both 2b = a + c and b = (a + c)/2
questions.rationale: In AP, middle term equals average of other two.

questions.question_text: What is the sum of AP: 1 + 2 + 3 + ... + 100?
If a₁ = 3, d = -2, what is a₆?

modules.explanation: Geometric progressions are like compound interest - each term is multiplied by the same ratio! Starting with 2 and multiplying by 3 each time: 2, 6, 18, 54, 162... that's a GP! GPs model exponential growth like population growth, compound interest, or bacterial growth. The nth term formula arⁿ⁻¹ helps calculate any term. GPs grow much faster than APs - that's why compound interest beats simple interest!

questions.question_text: What is a geometric progression?
questions.rationale: GP has constant ratio between consecutive terms.

questions.question_text: What is the common ratio of GP: 3, 6, 12, 24?
What is the 5th term of GP: 2, 6, 18, 54...?
Which sequence is a GP?
If a₁ = 5 and r = 2, what is a₄?
What is the formula for nth term of GP?
questions.rationale: Standard formula for nth term of geometric progression.

questions.question_text: If a₁ = 2 and a₅ = 162, what is r?
What type of GP has |r| < 1?
questions.rationale: When |r| < 1, GP converges to 0 as n increases.

questions.question_text: What is the sum to infinity of GP: 8, 4, 2, 1...?
If three terms of GP are a, b, c, what is the relationship?
questions.rationale: In GP, middle term squared equals product of other two.

questions.question_text: What is the sum of first 5 terms of GP: 1, 2, 4, 8, 16?
If a₁ = 1 and r = -2, what is a₄?
What happens to terms of GP when r > 1?
questions.rationale: When r > 1, terms grow exponentially larger.

questions.question_text: What is the geometric mean of 4 and 9?
If GP has sum to infinity 20 and first term 10, what is r?

modules.explanation: Quadratic equations are like finding the perfect balance! They have the form ax² + bx + c = 0 and can have 0, 1, or 2 real solutions. Think of throwing a ball - the path follows a parabola (quadratic), and the equation tells where it hits the ground! The quadratic formula x = (-b ± √(b²-4ac))/2a is like a master key that unlocks any quadratic equation. Discriminant b²-4ac tells us how many solutions exist!

questions.question_text: What is the standard form of quadratic equation?
questions.rationale: Standard form is ax² + bx + c = 0 where a ≠ 0.

questions.question_text: What is the discriminant of ax² + bx + c = 0?
questions.rationale: Discriminant Δ = b² - 4ac determines the nature of roots.

questions.question_text: If discriminant > 0, how many real roots does the equation have?
questions.rationale: When discriminant > 0, there are two distinct real roots.

questions.question_text: What is the quadratic formula?
questions.rationale: Quadratic formula provides solution for any quadratic equation.

questions.question_text: Solve: x² - 5x + 6 = 0
What is the sum of roots of 2x² - 8x + 6 = 0?
What is the product of roots of 3x² + 6x - 9 = 0?
Complete the square: x² + 6x + ?
If discriminant = 0, what type of roots?
questions.rationale: When discriminant = 0, there is exactly one real root (repeated).

questions.question_text: Solve: x² - 9 = 0
What is the vertex form of parabola?
If a quadratic opens upward, what is true about 'a'?
questions.rationale: When a > 0, parabola opens upward (minimum point).

questions.question_text: What is the axis of symmetry of x² - 4x + 3 = 0?
How many real roots does x² + 4x + 5 = 0 have?
What is the minimum value of x² - 6x + 5?

modules.explanation: Similar triangles are like photocopies of different sizes! They have the same shape but different sizes - corresponding angles are equal and sides are proportional. Think of a small triangle and its enlarged version - they're similar! The ratio of corresponding sides is constant. This concept helps in measuring heights of buildings, map scaling, and optical instruments. AAA, SAS, and SSS similarity criteria help identify similar triangles!

questions.question_text: What makes two triangles similar?
questions.rationale: Similar triangles have equal corresponding angles and proportional sides.

questions.question_text: If ΔABC ~ ΔDEF and AB = 6, DE = 9, what is the similarity ratio?
Which is NOT a similarity criterion?
questions.rationale: SSA is not a valid similarity criterion.

questions.question_text: If similar triangles have sides in ratio 2:5, what is the ratio of their areas?
What does AAA similarity mean?
options: Two sides proportional and included angle equal
questions.rationale: AAA means all three corresponding angles are equal.

questions.question_text: In similar triangles, corresponding sides are:
questions.rationale: Corresponding sides of similar triangles are proportional.

questions.question_text: If ΔABC ~ ΔDEF with ratio 3:4 and BC = 12, what is EF?
What is SAS similarity criterion?
options: Two sides proportional and included angle equal
questions.correct_answer: Two sides proportional and included angle equal
questions.rationale: SAS similarity requires two sides in proportion and included angle equal.

questions.question_text: If similar triangles have perimeters 15 cm and 25 cm, what is the similarity ratio?
Which triangles are always similar?
questions.rationale: All equilateral triangles have 60° angles, so they're always similar.

questions.question_text: What is the basic proportionality theorem?
options: Line parallel to one side divides other sides proportionally
Areas of similar triangles are proportional
questions.correct_answer: Line parallel to one side divides other sides proportionally
questions.rationale: Basic proportionality theorem (Thales) states this property.

questions.question_text: If similar triangles have area ratio 9:16, what is the side ratio?
Two right triangles with one acute angle equal are:
questions.rationale: AA similarity - if one acute angle equal, other must also be equal.

questions.question_text: What is SSS similarity criterion?
options: Two sides equal and included angle equal
questions.rationale: SSS similarity requires all three corresponding sides to be proportional.

questions.question_text: If ΔABC ~ ΔDEF and AB:BC = 2:3, what is DE:EF?
questions.rationale: Corresponding sides have the same ratio in similar triangles.

modules.explanation: Coordinate geometry is like giving every point an address! The distance formula helps find how far apart two points are: √[(x₂-x₁)² + (y₂-y₁)²]. Think of it as the Pythagorean theorem in coordinate form! The section formula helps find points that divide lines in given ratios - like finding the midpoint (special case with ratio 1:1). These tools help in navigation, computer graphics, and GPS systems!

questions.question_text: What is the distance between (1,2) and (4,6)?
What is the midpoint of (2,4) and (6,8)?
What is the distance formula between (x₁,y₁) and (x₂,y₂)?
questions.rationale: Distance formula derived from Pythagorean theorem.

questions.question_text: If a point divides (1,2) and (7,8) in ratio 2:1, what are its coordinates?
What is the distance from origin to (3,4)?
Which points are collinear: (1,1), (2,2), (3,4)?
questions.rationale: Check slope: (2,2) to (1,1) has slope 1, (3,4) to (2,2) has slope 2. Different slopes.

questions.question_text: What is the centroid of triangle with vertices (0,0), (6,0), (0,6)?
What is the area of triangle with vertices (0,0), (4,0), (0,3)?
Which point lies on line x + y = 5?
What is the distance between (-2,1) and (3,-4)?
If a point divides (0,0) and (6,8) in ratio 1:2, what are its coordinates?
What is the slope of line through (1,2) and (3,8)?
Which point is equidistant from (0,0) and (6,0)?
questions.rationale: Midpoint of (0,0) and (6,0) is (3,0), equidistant from both.

questions.question_text: What is the distance between parallel lines y = 2x + 1 and y = 2x + 7?
If three points are collinear, what is true about the area of triangle formed?
questions.rationale: Collinear points lie on a straight line, so triangle area is zero.

modules.explanation: Trigonometry is the mathematics of triangles and angles! Sin, cos, and tan are like special ratios that relate angles to side lengths. Think of standing at a distance from a building - trigonometry helps calculate its height using just angles and distances! The fundamental identity sin²θ + cos²θ = 1 is like the Pythagorean theorem in trigonometric form. These concepts are crucial in navigation, engineering, and physics!

questions.question_text: What is sin²θ + cos²θ equal to?
What is tan 45°?
If sin θ = 3/5, what is cos θ?
questions.rationale: sin²θ + cos²θ = 1. (3/5)² + cos²θ = 1. 9/25 + cos²θ = 1. cos²θ = 16/25. cos θ = 4/5.

questions.question_text: What is the value of cos 0°?
What is sin 30°?
If tan θ = 3/4, what is sec θ?
questions.rationale: tan θ = opposite/adjacent = 3/4. hypotenuse = 5. sec θ = hypotenuse/adjacent = 5/4.

questions.question_text: What is 1 + tan²θ equal to?
If cos θ = 12/13, what is sin θ?
What is the value of sin 90°?
If a building casts a shadow of 20m and sun angle is 30°, what is the height?
What is cos 60°?
If sin θ = 4/5, what is cosec θ?
What is tan 60°?
If cot θ = 3/4, what is tan θ?
From a point 50m from tower, angle of elevation is 45°. What is tower height?

modules.explanation: Mensuration is like measuring the world around us! Surface area is like wrapping paper - how much material needed to cover a 3D shape. Volume is like capacity - how much a container can hold. For a cylinder: surface area = 2πr(r+h) and volume = πr²h. These formulas help in construction, packaging, and manufacturing. Think of calculating paint needed for a tank or water it can hold!

questions.question_text: What is the volume of a cube with side 6 cm?
What is the curved surface area of cylinder with radius 3 cm and height 5 cm?
What is the total surface area of cuboid 4cm × 3cm × 2cm?
What is the volume of cylinder with radius 7 cm and height 10 cm?
What is the surface area of cube with side 5 cm?
If a sphere has volume 36π cm³, what is its radius?
What is the volume of cone with radius 6 cm and height 8 cm?
What is the total surface area of cylinder with radius 4 cm and height 6 cm?
If radius of sphere is doubled, how does surface area change?
questions.rationale: Surface area = 4πr². If r becomes 2r, area = 4π(2r)² = 4 × 4πr² = 4 times.

questions.question_text: What is the volume of hemisphere with radius 3 cm?
What is the curved surface area of cone with radius 5 cm and slant height 13 cm?
If volume of cube is 1000 cm³, what is its surface area?
What is the ratio of volumes of two spheres with radii 2:3?
What is the surface area of sphere with radius 7 cm?
If a cylinder and cone have same radius and height, what is the ratio of their volumes?
questions.rationale: Volume cylinder = πr²h, Volume cone = (1/3)πr²h. Ratio = πr²h : (1/3)πr²h = 3:1.

modules.explanation: Statistics helps us make sense of data! Mean is the average, median is the middle value, and mode is most frequent. Think of test scores - mean tells overall performance, median shows typical score, mode reveals common patterns. Probability is like predicting the future - chance of events happening from 0 (impossible) to 1 (certain). These concepts help in weather forecasting, insurance, games, and decision-making!

questions.question_text: What is the mean of 2, 4, 6, 8, 10?
What is the median of 3, 1, 4, 2, 5?
What is the mode of 2, 3, 2, 4, 2, 5?
What is the probability of getting heads when tossing a fair coin?
questions.rationale: Fair coin has 2 equally likely outcomes. P(heads) = 1/2.

questions.question_text: What is the range of 5, 8, 3, 9, 2?
If mean of 5 numbers is 12, what is their sum?
What is the probability of getting a number greater than 4 when rolling a die?
What is the variance of 1, 2, 3, 4, 5?
What is the median of 2, 4, 6, 8?
If P(A) = 0.3, what is P(not A)?
What is the standard deviation of data with variance 9?
What is the probability of getting an even number when rolling a die?
If two events are mutually exclusive, what is P(A or B)?
questions.rationale: For mutually exclusive events: P(A ∪ B) = P(A) + P(B).

questions.question_text: What is the mode of 1, 2, 3, 4, 5?
questions.rationale: All numbers appear exactly once, so there is no mode.

questions.question_text: If P(A) = 0.4 and P(B) = 0.5 and A, B are independent, what is P(A and B)?
questions.rationale: For independent events: P(A ∩ B) = P(A) × P(B) = 0.4 × 0.5 = 0.2.
